summary:
Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BC) was the most influential thinker in Chinese history — a teacher, philosopher, and moral reformer whose ideas about ethics, governance, family relationships, and self-cultivation shaped Chinese civilization for over 2,500 years and continue to influence East Asian cultures today. Living during the turbulent Spring and Autumn Period when the Zhou political order was fragmenting, Confucius developed a philosophical response to political disorder centered on the recovery of proper ritual observance, moral self-cultivation, and humane governance.

The core of Confucian thought centers on ren (benevolence, humaneness) — the quality of genuine concern for others expressed through ritual propriety (li) in all relationships. Confucius analyzed society through the 'five relationships' (ruler-subject, father-son, husband-wife, elder brother-younger brother, friend-friend), arguing that each carries specific obligations and that social harmony flows from each person fulfilling their relational duties with genuine virtue. He insisted that legitimate authority rested on moral virtue rather than force: the superior man (junzi) governs by moral example, causing the people to be virtuous through emulation.

Confucius spent years as a wandering advisor seeking a ruler who would implement his vision of good government, apparently without sustained success; his influence came primarily through his students and the texts compiled after his death (the Analects). His ideas were marginalized in the competitive Warring States era by Legalism's more effective techniques, but were elevated to state orthodoxy under the Han dynasty (see [[event:1131|Confucianism as State Ideology]]) and remained the theoretical foundation of Chinese imperial governance, civil service examination culture, and family ethics for two millennia.
